macro_segments.title: Introduction to Kinnuyim: Indirect References to Korban

micro_segments.title: Definition and examples of kinnuyim as substitute terminology
The mishna's list: non-chulin, tamei, notar, pigul as korban references
Objects associated with the altar: sheep, pens, wood, fire, mizbeach, heichal, Yerushalayim
Rabbi Yehudah's distinction: Yerushalayim alone versus ki-Yerushalayim

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Initial Assumption: Miklal Lav and Rabbi Meir's Logical Framework

micro_segments.title: The Gemara assumes la-chulin means korban via miklal lav ata shomea hen
Rabbi Meir's principle: cannot infer the opposite without explicit statement
The example of Bnei Gad and Bnei Reuven: why both sides must be stated
Two interpretations of Rabbi Meir: speech acts versus logical necessity
Propositional logic: if P then Q does not entail if not P then not Q

macro_segments.title: Identifying the Mishnah's Author and the Distinction Between Yad and Kinnuy

micro_segments.title: The Gemara initially suggests the mishnah follows Rabbi Meir
Tosafot Rid's critique: la-chulin is definitional, not inferential
Yad versus kinnuy: filling blanks versus interpreting words
Ran's theory: tamei, notar, pigul foreground issur status; require no ki
Objects like mizbeach, Yerushalayim need ki to create meaningful comparison

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehudah's Position: Ki Yerushalayim versus Yerushalayim Alone

micro_segments.title: Gemara reasserts: entire mishnah is Rabbi Yehudah's position
Rabbi Yehudah makes distinction: ki-Yerushalayim works, Yerushalayim alone does not
Yerushalayim too broad: includes Knesset, museums, Biblical Zoo

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehudah's Position: Ki Yerushalayim versus Yerushalayim Alone (continued)

micro_segments.title: Braita introduces stricter view: even ki-Yerushalayim requires explicit korban reference

macro_segments.title: La-Chulin, Le-Korban, and the Question of Conditional Statements

micro_segments.title: Braita: chulin, ha-chulin, ka-chulin are all non-binding
La-chulin she-ochal lach: forbidden because la-chulin means korban
Tosafot Rid rejects miklal lav here: la-chulin is definitional synonym
Gemara distinguishes between conditional propositions and direct naming
La-chulin lo ochal lach: permitted because non-eating is abstract

macro_segments.title: Le-Korban Lo Ochal Lach and the Reinterpretation Strategy

micro_segments.title: Gemara introduces later mishna: le-korban lo ochal lach is forbidden for Rabbi Meir
Rav Abba's solution: read le-korban as lekorban—making the thing itself a korban
Gemara asks: if la-chulin means korban, why not apply same logic?
This braita author holds Rabbi Meir's position but not the chopping strategy
Answer: la-chulin lo ochal lach refers to abstract non-eating concept

macro_segments.title: She-Ani vs. She-Eini Ochal Lach: Objects versus Actions in Neder Language

micro_segments.title: Computer and car example: cannot make arbitrary non-foods forbidden
Konam she-ani ochal lach: the food is prohibited, therefore I won't eat
Lashon neder requires focusing on the object, not the speaker's action
Dispute: is she-eini ochal lach binding or does it reference action rather than object?

macro_segments.title: Tahor, Tamei, Notar, Pigul: Korban Statuses in New Context

micro_segments.title: New section begins: tahor, tamei, notar, pigul asur
Question: what if bread is declared like shelamim meat after blood-throwing?
If meat is explicitly in its permitted state, different status applies
Gemara scenario: person eating shelamim holds it up; another declares bread like this meat
Central question: be-ikaro ka-matpis or be-heiter ka-matpis—original vs. current status

topical_tags.term: Miklal lav ata shomea hen (inferring opposites from statements)
Rabbi Meir vs. Sages (conditional logic dispute)
Be-ikaro vs. be-heiter (original versus current status)
Tosafot Rid (medieval commentary differentiating defintion from inference)
She-ani ochal lach vs. she-eini ochal lach
Bnei Gad and Bnei Reuven (scriptural precedent for explicit conditions)
